questions.subject: Physics
questions.skill: Describe the structure of an atom
questions.question: A learner uses a classroom atom model to check the key physics idea. Which statement about the structure of an atom is correct?
options: Electrons are found inside protons in the nucleus.
Neutrons have a positive charge.
The nucleus contains only electrons.
Protons and neutrons are found in the nucleus.
questions.correctAnswer: Protons and neutrons are found in the nucleus.
questions.explanation: The nucleus contains protons and neutrons. Electrons move around the nucleus.
questions.senNote: The nucleus contains protons and neutrons. Electrons move around the nucleus.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.skill: Describe the structure of an atom
questions.question: A learner uses a revision diagram of a lithium atom to check the key physics idea. Which statement about the structure of an atom is correct?
options: Electrons are found inside protons in the nucleus.
Neutrons have a positive charge.
Protons and neutrons are found in the nucleus.
Protons are found in shells around the nucleus.
questions.correctAnswer: Protons and neutrons are found in the nucleus.
questions.explanation: The nucleus contains protons and neutrons. Electrons move around the nucleus.
questions.senNote: The nucleus contains protons and neutrons. Electrons move around the nucleus.

options: Electrons are found inside protons in the nucleus.

Neutrons move around the outside of the atom.
questions.correctAnswer: Protons and neutrons are found in the nucleus.
questions.explanation: The nucleus contains protons and neutrons. Electrons move around the nucleus.
questions.senNote: The nucleus contains protons and neutrons. Electrons move around the nucleus.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.skill: Describe the structure of an atom

options: Protons and neutrons are found in the nucleus.

Neutrons have a positive charge.
Electrons have the same positive charge as protons.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A learner uses a Geiger counter demonstration to check the key physics idea. What is background radiation?
options: Radiation produced only in nuclear power stations.
Radiation that appears only when a source is switched on.
Radiation that is present only in science laboratories.
The low level of radiation that is always present in the environment.
questions.correctAnswer: The low level of radiation that is always present in the environment.
questions.explanation: Background radiation comes from natural and artificial sources around us.
questions.senNote: Background radiation comes from natural and artificial sources around us.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A learner uses a classroom radiation survey to check the key physics idea. What is background radiation?
options: Radiation produced only in nuclear power stations.
Radiation that appears only when a source is switched on.
The low level of radiation that is always present in the environment.
Radiation that means an object has become radioactive forever.
questions.correctAnswer: The low level of radiation that is always present in the environment.
questions.explanation: Background radiation comes from natural and artificial sources around us.
questions.senNote: Background radiation comes from natural and artificial sources around us.

questions.correctAnswer: The low level of radiation that is always present in the environment.
questions.explanation: Background radiation comes from natural and artificial sources around us.
questions.senNote: Background radiation comes from natural and artificial sources around us.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A learner uses a nuclear power station diagram to check the key physics idea. Which statement about nuclear fission is correct?
options: Electrons split out of atoms and cause chemical burning.
A neutral atom turns into a charged atom only because it gains heat.
The outer electron shells split and release gravitational energy.
A large nucleus splits into smaller nuclei and releases energy.
questions.correctAnswer: A large nucleus splits into smaller nuclei and releases energy.
questions.explanation: In nuclear fission, a large unstable nucleus splits and releases energy.
questions.senNote: In nuclear fission, a large unstable nucleus splits and releases energy.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A learner uses a star-energy explanation to check the key physics idea. Which statement about nuclear fission is correct?
options: Electrons split out of atoms and cause chemical burning.
A neutral atom turns into a charged atom only because it gains heat.
A large nucleus splits into smaller nuclei and releases energy.
A large nucleus cools down and becomes chemically neutral.
questions.correctAnswer: A large nucleus splits into smaller nuclei and releases energy.
questions.explanation: In nuclear fission, a large unstable nucleus splits and releases energy.
questions.senNote: In nuclear fission, a large unstable nucleus splits and releases energy.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A learner uses a reactor fuel description to check the key physics idea. Which statement about nuclear fission is correct?
options: Electrons split out of atoms and cause chemical burning.
A large nucleus splits into smaller nuclei and releases energy.
A neutral atom turns into a charged atom only because it gains heat.
Two atoms rub together and release sound energy.
questions.correctAnswer: A large nucleus splits into smaller nuclei and releases energy.
questions.explanation: In nuclear fission, a large unstable nucleus splits and releases energy.
questions.senNote: In nuclear fission, a large unstable nucleus splits and releases energy.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A learner uses a fusion research note to check the key physics idea. Which statement about nuclear fission is correct?
options: A large nucleus splits into smaller nuclei and releases energy.
Electrons split out of atoms and cause chemical burning.
A neutral atom turns into a charged atom only because it gains heat.
The nucleus loses all of its protons but stays the same element.
questions.correctAnswer: A large nucleus splits into smaller nuclei and releases energy.
questions.explanation: In nuclear fission, a large unstable nucleus splits and releases energy.
questions.senNote: In nuclear fission, a large unstable nucleus splits and releases energy.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A learner uses a radiographer safety poster to check the key physics idea. Which statement correctly compares contamination and irradiation?
options: Contamination and irradiation both stop as soon as a lead sheet is used.
Both words mean the same thing in radiation safety.
Irradiation always leaves radioactive atoms inside the object.
Contamination means unwanted radioactive material is on or in an object, but irradiation means exposure to radiation only.
questions.correctAnswer: Contamination means unwanted radioactive material is on or in an object, but irradiation means exposure to radiation only.
questions.explanation: Irradiation does not make an object radioactive. Contamination involves radioactive substances being present on or in the object.
questions.senNote: Irradiation does not make an object radioactive. Contamination involves radioactive substances being present on or in the object.

questions.explanation: Irradiation does not make an object radioactive. Contamination involves radioactive substances being present on or in the object.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.skill: Describe the resistance of a filament lamp
questions.question: A learner uses a filament lamp graph to check the key physics idea. What happens to the resistance of a filament lamp when the current through it increases?
options: Its resistance falls to zero because the lamp glows.
Its resistance stays constant because it is ohmic.
Its resistance changes only if the potential difference is zero.
Its resistance increases because the filament gets hotter.
questions.correctAnswer: Its resistance increases because the filament gets hotter.
questions.explanation: As the filament lamp gets hotter, its resistance increases.
questions.senNote: As the filament lamp gets hotter, its resistance increases.

Its resistance stays constant because it is ohmic.
Its resistance increases because the filament gets hotter.
The potential difference is used up inside the filament.
questions.correctAnswer: Its resistance increases because the filament gets hotter.
questions.explanation: As the filament lamp gets hotter, its resistance increases.
questions.senNote: As the filament lamp gets hotter, its resistance increases.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.skill: Explain the purpose of a fuse
questions.question: A learner uses a three-pin plug diagram to check the key physics idea. Why is a fuse placed in the live wire of a plug?
options: It keeps the neutral wire at a higher voltage.
It increases the current to start the appliance.
It stores charge so the appliance works longer.
It melts and breaks the circuit if the current becomes too large.
questions.correctAnswer: It melts and breaks the circuit if the current becomes too large.
questions.explanation: A fuse is a safety device. It melts if the current is too large and cuts off the supply.
questions.senNote: A fuse is a safety device. It melts if the current is too large and cuts off the supply.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.skill: Explain the purpose of a fuse
questions.question: A learner uses a home wiring safety check to check the key physics idea. Why is a fuse placed in the live wire of a plug?
options: It keeps the neutral wire at a higher voltage.
It increases the current to start the appliance.
It melts and breaks the circuit if the current becomes too large.
It protects the appliance by making the earth wire live.
questions.correctAnswer: It melts and breaks the circuit if the current becomes too large.
questions.explanation: A fuse is a safety device. It melts if the current is too large and cuts off the supply.
questions.senNote: A fuse is a safety device. It melts if the current is too large and cuts off the supply.

questions.question: A learner uses a household energy bill to check the key physics idea. A 2 kW heater runs for 3 hours. Electricity costs 25 p per kWh. What is the cost?

questions.subject: Physics
questions.skill: Recall potential difference in a parallel circuit
questions.question: A learner uses a parallel lamp circuit to check the key physics idea. Which statement about potential difference in a parallel circuit is correct?
options: The supply voltage becomes zero in each branch.
The current is the same in each branch because the voltage changes.
The voltage is shared equally only in a series circuit.
The potential difference across each branch is the same as the supply.
questions.correctAnswer: The potential difference across each branch is the same as the supply.
questions.explanation: In a parallel circuit, each branch gets the full supply potential difference.
questions.senNote: In a parallel circuit, each branch gets the full supply potential difference.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.skill: Recall potential difference in a parallel circuit
questions.question: A learner uses a two-branch circuit diagram to check the key physics idea. Which statement about potential difference in a parallel circuit is correct?
options: The supply voltage becomes zero in each branch.
The current is the same in each branch because the voltage changes.
The potential difference across each branch is the same as the supply.
Each branch receives half the supply voltage automatically.
questions.correctAnswer: The potential difference across each branch is the same as the supply.
questions.explanation: In a parallel circuit, each branch gets the full supply potential difference.
questions.senNote: In a parallel circuit, each branch gets the full supply potential difference.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A learner uses a road safety lesson to check the key physics idea. A car travels on tired driver. Which answer is best?
options: Only thinking distance changes; braking distance is fixed.
The car stops instantly when the driver sees danger.
Wet roads reduce stopping distance in every case.
The thinking distance increases because reactions are slower.
questions.correctAnswer: The thinking distance increases because reactions are slower.
questions.explanation: Stopping distance depends on thinking distance and braking distance. Grip and reaction time both matter.
questions.senNote: Stopping distance depends on thinking distance and braking distance. Grip and reaction time both matter.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A learner uses a driver reaction scenario to check the key physics idea. A car travels on worn tyres. Which answer is best?
options: Only thinking distance changes; braking distance is fixed.

The braking distance increases because grip is reduced.
Higher speed always reduces braking distance.

questions.explanation: Stopping distance depends on thinking distance and braking distance. Grip and reaction time both matter.
questions.senNote: Stopping distance depends on thinking distance and braking distance. Grip and reaction time both matter.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A learner uses a generator demonstration to check the key physics idea. What is electromagnetic induction?
options: A conductor becomes a permanent magnet whenever a battery is removed.
A current is made by heating a wire until it glows.
A magnetic field is destroyed when iron is placed near it.
A potential difference is produced when a conductor cuts through magnetic field lines.
questions.correctAnswer: A potential difference is produced when a conductor cuts through magnetic field lines.
questions.explanation: Electromagnetic induction happens when a conductor experiences a changing magnetic field.
questions.senNote: Electromagnetic induction happens when a conductor experiences a changing magnetic field.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A learner uses a simple motor diagram to check the key physics idea. What causes the motor effect?
options: A magnetic field always stops current flowing in a conductor.
The conductor gains mass when current passes through it.

A current-carrying conductor experiences a force in a magnetic field.

questions.explanation: The magnetic field around the conductor interacts with the external magnetic field and produces a force.
questions.senNote: The magnetic field around the conductor interacts with the external magnetic field and produces a force.

questions.question: A learner uses a gas syringe compression to check the key physics idea. A gas is trapped in a syringe and compressed. What happens to the pressure?

questions.explanation: With less space, particles hit the container walls more often, so pressure rises.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A learner uses a boiling-water graph to check the key physics idea. On a heating curve, what happens while the substance is boiling?
options: The temperature rises quickly throughout boiling.
The substance becomes colder while it boils.
The particles stop moving during boiling.
The temperature stays constant while energy is transferred to separate the particles.

questions.explanation: During boiling, energy is used to overcome forces between particles rather than increase temperature.
questions.senNote: During boiling, energy is used to overcome forces between particles rather than increase temperature.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.skill: Explain why satellites stay in orbit
questions.question: A learner uses a satellite orbit diagram to check the key physics idea. Why does a satellite stay in orbit around Earth?
options: Air resistance above Earth pushes the satellite around the orbit.
The satellite engine must keep firing all the time to hold the orbit.
There is no gravity in space, so the satellite floats in a straight line.
Gravity provides the centripetal force needed to keep the satellite moving in a curved path.
questions.correctAnswer: Gravity provides the centripetal force needed to keep the satellite moving in a curved path.
questions.explanation: Gravity constantly pulls the satellite towards Earth, changing its direction and keeping it in orbit.
questions.senNote: Gravity constantly pulls the satellite towards Earth, changing its direction and keeping it in orbit.

questions.skill: Identify a use of geostationary satellites
questions.question: A learner uses a communications satellite case to check the key physics idea. Which is a common use of geostationary satellites?
options: To measure the mass of atoms in the air.
To stop gravity acting on objects.
To make planets move faster.
Satellite television and communications
questions.correctAnswer: Satellite television and communications
questions.explanation: Geostationary satellites stay above the same point on Earth, making them useful for communications and broadcasting.
questions.senNote: Geostationary satellites stay above the same point on Earth, making them useful for communications and broadcasting.

questions.skill: Describe the image formed by a convex lens
questions.question: A learner uses a convex lens ray diagram to check the key physics idea. A convex lens forms an image of a distant object. Which description is correct?
options: The image is always virtual and behind the lens.
The rays never meet after passing through the lens.
The focal point is on the same side as the object.
The image is real and can be focused onto a screen.
questions.correctAnswer: The image is real and can be focused onto a screen.
questions.explanation: A convex lens can form a real image that can be projected onto a screen when the object is beyond the focal length.
questions.senNote: A convex lens can form a real image that can be projected onto a screen when the object is beyond the focal length.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.skill: Describe the image formed by a convex lens
questions.question: A learner uses a camera lens explanation to prepare for an exam-style question. A convex lens forms an image of a distant object. Which description is correct? Choose the best answer.
options: The image is always virtual and behind the lens.
The rays never meet after passing through the lens.
The image is real and can be focused onto a screen.
Parallel rays pass through without changing direction.
questions.correctAnswer: The image is real and can be focused onto a screen.
questions.explanation: A convex lens can form a real image that can be projected onto a screen when the object is beyond the focal length.
questions.senNote: A convex lens can form a real image that can be projected onto a screen when the object is beyond the focal length.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A learner uses a plane mirror ray diagram to check the key physics idea. A ray of light hits a mirror at an angle of incidence of 25°. What is the angle of reflection?
options: The angle of reflection is always zero.
The angle of reflection is twice the angle of incidence.
The angle is measured from the mirror surface only.
questions.explanation: The angle of reflection equals the angle of incidence.
questions.senNote: The angle of reflection equals the angle of incidence.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A learner uses a light ray practical to check the key physics idea. A ray of light hits a mirror at an angle of incidence of 30°. What is the angle of reflection?
options: The angle of reflection is always zero.
The angle of reflection is twice the angle of incidence.
The reflected ray travels back along the normal.
questions.explanation: The angle of reflection equals the angle of incidence.
questions.senNote: The angle of reflection equals the angle of incidence.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A learner uses a speaker amplitude demonstration to check the key physics idea. Which change makes a sound louder?
options: Decrease the amplitude.
Decrease the frequency.
Increase the wavelength only.
Increasing the amplitude of the wave
questions.correctAnswer: Increasing the amplitude of the wave
questions.explanation: Loudness depends on amplitude. A larger amplitude means more energy is transferred.
questions.senNote: Loudness depends on amplitude. A larger amplitude means more energy is transferred.

options: Decrease the amplitude.

questions.explanation: Loudness depends on amplitude. A larger amplitude means more energy is transferred.
questions.senNote: Loudness depends on amplitude. A larger amplitude means more energy is transferred.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A learner uses a slinky wave demonstration to check the key physics idea. Which statement about transverse and longitudinal waves is correct?
options: All waves are transverse in air.
Sound waves are transverse and light waves are longitudinal.
Light waves need particles to carry them.
Light waves are transverse, but sound waves in air are longitudinal.
questions.correctAnswer: Light waves are transverse, but sound waves in air are longitudinal.
questions.explanation: Light is a transverse electromagnetic wave. Sound in air is longitudinal.
questions.senNote: Light is a transverse electromagnetic wave. Sound in air is longitudinal.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A learner uses a sound and light comparison to check the key physics idea. Which statement about transverse and longitudinal waves is correct?
options: All waves are transverse in air.
Sound waves are transverse and light waves are longitudinal.
Light waves are transverse, but sound waves in air are longitudinal.
All waves must travel through a solid.
questions.correctAnswer: Light waves are transverse, but sound waves in air are longitudinal.
questions.explanation: Light is a transverse electromagnetic wave. Sound in air is longitudinal.
questions.senNote: Light is a transverse electromagnetic wave. Sound in air is longitudinal.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A learner uses a wave-type sorting task to check the key physics idea. Which statement about transverse and longitudinal waves is correct?
options: All waves are transverse in air.
Light waves are transverse, but sound waves in air are longitudinal.
Sound waves are transverse and light waves are longitudinal.
Longitudinal waves have vibrations at right angles to travel.
questions.correctAnswer: Light waves are transverse, but sound waves in air are longitudinal.
questions.explanation: Light is a transverse electromagnetic wave. Sound in air is longitudinal.
questions.senNote: Light is a transverse electromagnetic wave. Sound in air is longitudinal.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A learner uses a transverse-longitudinal model to check the key physics idea. Which statement about transverse and longitudinal waves is correct?
options: Light waves are transverse, but sound waves in air are longitudinal.
All waves are transverse in air.
Sound waves are transverse and light waves are longitudinal.
Transverse waves vibrate parallel to their direction of travel.
